Paragraph
The formulation of Theorem 4.5.8 specifies a splitting of the variables \((\bx, \by)\text{.}\) In applications, one often has some flexibility in making a choice of the splitting. For example, suppose that \(f(x, y, z)\) has continuous partial derivatives, and one is interested in understanding the set \(\{(x, y, z): f(x, y, z)=f(x_{0}, y_{0},z_{0})\}\) for \((x, y, z)\) near \((x_{0}, y_{0},z_{0})\)---this is called a level set of \(f\text{.}\) \(f(x, y, z)\) here is a scalar function so any application of Theorem 4.5.8 is to solve for one of the variables in terms of the remaining two.
in-context
